learning_outcomes.description: Describe factors that influence communication, and how individual differences influence communication outcomes and group collaboration
Compare methods available to facilitate group interaction, planning and action.
Apply the principles and processes of group development and dynamics
Explain group processes and negotiation skills in group problem solving and conflict management situations
Appraise what makes an effective group member and leader within diverse team settings

assessments.category: Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Participation/ Student contribution
assessments.task_description:
This assessment brings together two capabilities that are often treated separately but are deeply connected in practice: the ability to analyse how a group functions, and the ability to reflect honestly on your own role within teams and groups. The best food and agricultural professionals and sustainability practitioners are not just technically competent — they can read a group situation clearly and understand what their own behaviour contributes to it.

Assessment 1 is a single integrated written report with two distinct parts. Both parts must be present in your submission for it to be considered complete. The total submitted word count is 1,800–2,300 words, broken down as follows:

Part A is a structured analytical report in which you select a real group from your professional or personal experience and apply course frameworks from Modules 1 and 2 to examine how the group has developed, how individual differences have shaped its communication, what dysfunction has occurred, and what specific improvements you would recommend. Part A has five required sections; see the Part A structure guide on Blackboard for the specific sections and their word allocations.

Part B is a 600–800 word personal reflection written in response to three guided prompts, synthesising the thinking you have accumulated across the semester. Throughout the semester you will maintain a structured reflective journal, responding to module-linked prompts released on Blackboard at the start of each module. These prompts are designed to help you connect course content to your own experiences, observations, and developing understanding. Your journal must be attached as an appendix to this assessment document, providing evidence of your engagement with the course across the semester. Although attached to your submission, the journal itself does not receive a separate mark — it is read only in support of your Part B reflection, and there is no prescribed word length for individual journal entries. Markers will consider whether your Part B reflection is consistent with, and clearly developed from, the journal entries provided; reflections that do not draw evidently on sustained engagement across the semester will not score as highly as those that do. Part B is not a task you begin at the end of semester — it is a distillation of reflection you have been building throughout the course.

The two parts are submitted as a single document. Use clear headings to separate them. Your reference list appears at the end of the document after Part B, covers both parts, and is excluded from the total word count.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

The use of AI or MT must be referenced appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The two parts are submitted as a single document. You must submit your Assessment task, in Blackboard, via the correct link by the submission deadline. You should also retain an electronic copy of every piece of assessment you submit.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: In-person Group Facilitation Simulation — Small Groups, Individually Assessed
assessments.due_date:
16/10/2026 - 23/10/2026

In class in weeks 11 & 12. You must attend your allocated session.
assessments.category: Participation/ Student contribution, Presentation, Role play/ Simulation
assessments.task_description:
Assessment 2 is a live, in-person facilitation exercise conducted during timetabled sessions in Weeks 11 and 12 at the Gatton campus. You must attend your allocated session. It cannot be completed online or submitted as a written task.

Your scenario briefing and role card will be released on Blackboard 48 hours before your session — it is your responsibility to read these in advance. If you are unable to attend due to illness or exceptional circumstances, you must notify the course coordinator as early as possible and provide supporting documentation (such as a medical certificate). Alternative sessions will be arranged only where documentation is provided and circumstances are genuinely exceptional. Students who do not attend their allocated session without approved documentation will receive no marks for this assessment.

Your performance is observed and marked in real time by the course coordinator and members of the teaching team, against a rubric covering four criteria: facilitation technique, conflict management, communication, and adaptability. See Blackboard for the full marking rubric.

You will be placed in a group of 4–5 students and assigned a role in a structured, realistic scenario. One student in the group acts as the primary facilitator; the remaining students play team members with defined professional backgrounds, interests, and positions relevant to the scenario. The scenario is set in a realistic agricultural, food industry, or natural resource management context. Students will receive their scenario briefing and role card on Blackboard 48 hours before their scheduled session. Further details of the assessment will be provided in Week 2.

Students are required to form their groups during Week 1. If a student has not joined or formed a group by Week 3, they should contact the course coordinator immediately so they can be placed in one. If a student has not made contact by Week 3, the course coordinator will assign them to a group in Week 4. Please note: students who do not join a group will be encouraged to withdraw from the course, as there are some assessments that are group-based. Your absence will not disadvantage other students in your group. The simulation session will proceed as planned with the remaining group members. Where a group is reduced to fewer than three students due to an approved absence, the course coordinator will make alternative arrangements for that session, which may include merging groups or rescheduling the session, or arranging for volunteers to support the group, so that all affected students can fully participate and be assessed fairly.

Students who are unable to attend their allocated session due to illness or exceptional circumstances must notify the course coordinator as early as possible and provide supporting documentation. Without approved documentation, no marks can be awarded for this assessment.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance.

A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

The use of AI or MT must be acknowledged appropriately. Please see assessment guidelines in blackboard course site for specific instructions. Students may also refer to Library guides found under Additional assessment information > Acknowledging and referencing AI use.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You must attend your allocated session. This assessment cannot be completed online or submitted as a written task.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.task_description:
Assessment 3 is a formal, closed-book, paper-based examination held in person on the UQ Gatton campus during the end-of-semester examination period. Content from all five course modules is examinable. Your exact exam date, time, and venue will be communicated by UQ Examinations and published in your personal exam timetable on my.UQ.

The exam tests something that the Integrated Report and Facilitation Simulation cannot: your ability to draw on the full body of course theory independently, under individual conditions, and apply it analytically to situations you have not seen before. When a difficult group dynamic emerges on a property, in a processing plant, or at a community meeting, you will not always have time to consult notes or colleagues. What you will have is your understanding — and the exam tests exactly that.

All students enrolled in this course must attend the exam in person. You are required to bring your UQ student ID card to the examination venue.

This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeksFrom Week 1 To Week 13(27 Jul - 01 Nov)
learning_activities.topic:
Lecture Series

All lectures run from 1pm to 2pm. No lectures in Weeks 11 and 12; applied workshops run instead.


Lectures 1 to 3 (Week 1 commencing 27/07/2026 - Week 3 commencing 10/08/2026)

Module 1: Self-Awareness, Personal Values, and Communication. Understanding Yourself to Work with Others


Lectures 4 to 6 (Week 4 commencing 17/08/2026 - Week 6 commencing 31/08/2026)

Module 2: Group and Team Dynamics and Collaborative Approaches. How They Form, Function, and Fail


Lectures 7 to 10 (Week 7 commencing 7/09/2026 - Week 10 commencing 5/10/2026)

Module 3: Facilitating Groups and Teams. Negotiation, Consensus Building, and Conflict Management


Mid-sem break commencing 28/09/2026


Applied workshops, no formal lecture (Week 11 commencing 12/10/2026 - Week 12 commencing 19/10/2026)

Module 4: Leadership. Developing and Sharing It


Lecture 11 (Week 13 commencing 26/10/2026)

Module 5: Integration and Looking Forward
